title: How do you calculate zakat on long-term investments like 401k and mutual funds?
stanceSummary: For long-term investments that you are not actively monitoring or trading (such as mutual funds and 401k accounts), zakat is due on one-third of the total value, not the full amount. So you pay 2.5% of one-third of the portfolio value. However, if you are a day trader actively buying and selling stocks, zakat is due on the full 100% of the portfolio value. This is the position of AMJA and other major Islamic finance councils.
transcript:
## Summary of Yasir Qadhi's Position

In a [2025 video](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fABBy2fBcIg) (starting at [3:44](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fABBy2fBcIg&t=224s)), Yasir Qadhi explained the zakat ruling on long-term investments, citing the fatwa from AMJA (Assembly of Muslim Jurists of America) and other councils.

- **Long-term investments** (mutual funds, 401k, index funds not actively traded): Zakat is due on **one-third** of the total value
- **Actively traded portfolios** (day trading, frequent buying/selling): Zakat is due on the **full 100%** of the value
- The zakat rate remains **2.5%** in both cases
- This means $100,000 in a long-term mutual fund has a zakatable amount of ~$33,000, making the zakat owed ~$825

---

## Full Lecture Transcript (Cleaned)

### The Question — [3:44](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fABBy2fBcIg&t=224s)

The question was about zakat on long-term investments -- mutual funds and similar assets that you are not monitoring day by day.

### The Ruling — [3:56](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fABBy2fBcIg&t=236s)

The fatwa from **AMJA** (Assembly of Muslim Jurists of America) and other councils is consistent on this matter: long-term mutual funds and investments that you are not monitoring day by day are **zakatable**, but not on the full amount.

The simple equation that many modern Islamic finance experts give is that because mutual funds and stocks are varied in nature, **zakat is due on one-third of that amount**, not the full amount.

### Practical Example — [4:49](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fABBy2fBcIg&t=289s)

If you have $100,000 in a mutual fund sitting there long-term, or even in a **401k** sitting there long-term, your zakat is due on **$33,000** (one-third), not on $100,000. So the actual zakat payment would be 2.5% of $33,000.

### Day Traders — [4:58](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fABBy2fBcIg&t=298s)

If you are a **day trader** -- you are monitoring your portfolio, buying, selling, seeing the value fluctuate, and actively waiting to sell and buy -- then zakat is due on the **full 100%** of the portfolio value.

### The Distinction — [5:07](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fABBy2fBcIg&t=307s)

The key distinction is whether you are passively holding investments for the long term or actively trading them. Passive, long-term holdings have a reduced zakatable base (one-third), while actively traded portfolios are fully zakatable.
alternateQuestions: How do I calculate zakat on my 401k?
Is zakat due on retirement accounts?
Do I pay zakat on mutual funds?
How much zakat on stock investments?
